era_context: The Hellenistic Age is beginning. Alexander III of Macedon has recently ascended to the throne (336 BCE) and is consolidating power in Greece and the Balkans. The Persian Achaemenid Empire under Darius III dominates Asia Minor, the Levant, Egypt, and Mesopotamia. Greek city-states remain fractious; Egyptian and Phoenician naval power is still significant. This is the threshold of one of history's most consequential military campaigns.

events.title: Alexander crosses the Hellespont and invades Asia Minor
events.region: Hellespont (Dardanelles Strait) / Phrygia, northwestern Asia Minor
events.description: Alexander III of Macedon, aged 22, leads an army of approximately 35,000 infantry and 5,000 cavalry across the Hellespont into Persian-controlled Asia Minor. This crossing marks the beginning of his campaign to conquer the Persian Empire. The crossing itself was unopposed; Persian forces under satraps Arsites, Spithridates, and Rheomithres were gathering but had not yet concentrated. Alexander's invasion was motivated by both personal ambition and the long-standing Greek desire to punish Persia for the invasions of 480–479 BCE. The crossing was a logistical and symbolic act of commitment—Alexander reportedly threw his spear onto Asian soil first, claiming it as a spear-won prize.
key_figures: Spithridates (Persian satrap of Lydia and Ionia)

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander (compiled 2nd century CE from earlier sources, particularly Ptolemy I and Aristobulus)
Plutarch, Life of Alexander (1st–2nd century CE)
Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca Historica (1st century BCE)
Curtius Rufus, History of Alexander the Great (1st century CE, fragmentary)

events.certainty_note: The crossing is attested in multiple near-contemporary sources compiled by historians who had access to eyewitness accounts (Ptolemy, Aristobulus). The date 334 BCE is secure in the Macedonian and Greek chronological tradition. No contemporary inscriptions or papyri directly record the event, but the consistency of the narrative across independent sources and the strategic logic of the campaign make the event itself certain.

events.title: Battle of the Granicus River
events.region: Granicus River valley, Phrygia, northwestern Asia Minor
events.description: Approximately two months after crossing the Hellespont, Alexander encounters a Persian force under the command of satraps Arsites, Spithridates, and others at the Granicus River. The Persian force is estimated at 20,000–40,000 troops (sources vary widely). Alexander's army, numbering ~35,000, attacks across the river in a bold frontal assault. The Macedonian cavalry, led by Alexander himself, breaks the Persian line. The Persian force is routed; casualties are reported as 1,000–20,000 (sources are unreliable). This victory is Alexander's first major engagement against Persian forces and establishes his reputation as a military commander. It opens the road to Sardis and the interior of Asia Minor. The battle demonstrated the superiority of Macedonian phalanx tactics and cavalry coordination over Persian forces, though the Persians had not yet committed their full strength.
key_figures: Arsites (Persian satrap, reportedly killed in battle)
Spithridates (Persian satrap, reportedly killed in battle)
Memnon of Rhodes (Greek mercenary commander, reportedly advised against the battle)
events.certainty_note: The battle is attested in all major ancient sources. The date (334 BCE, spring) is secure. However, casualty figures and tactical details vary significantly between sources. Modern scholarship accepts the battle as historical and strategically decisive, though exact numbers and some tactical claims (e.g., Alexander's near-death) are debated.

events.title: Siege and fall of Miletus
events.description: After the victory at Granicus, Alexander moves south along the Ionian coast, besieging the Greek city of Miletus, which had been garrisoned by Persian forces and Greek mercenaries. The city is defended by a Persian garrison under the command of Hegesistratus. Alexander's forces construct siege works and assault the city walls. The city falls after a brief siege (sources suggest days to weeks). The garrison is killed or enslaved. This siege is significant because it demonstrates Alexander's ability to conduct siege warfare and because it secures the Ionian coast for his advance. However, the Persian fleet remains a threat, and Alexander decides not to build a navy to match it, instead relying on land campaigns to eliminate Persian naval bases.
events.certainty_note: The siege is attested in multiple sources. The date (334 BCE, summer) is secure. Details of the siege are sparse in surviving accounts.
cross_references: Part of broader campaign to secure Ionian coast

events.title: Siege of Halicarnassus begins
events.description: Alexander moves to besiege Halicarnassus, a major fortified city and naval base in Caria, held by the Persian satrap Mausolus's widow Ada and a Persian garrison under Orontobates. The city is heavily fortified and defended by both land and sea forces. Alexander constructs siege works and assault towers. The siege is protracted and difficult; the city's defenders make multiple sorties. Eventually, the outer fortifications are breached, but the inner citadel holds out. The siege continues into 333 BCE. This siege is significant because Halicarnassus is a major Persian stronghold and because it demonstrates the difficulty of reducing fortified cities even for Alexander's army.
key_figures: Ada of Caria (widow of Mausolus, initially defending the city, later allied with Alexander)
events.certainty_note: The siege is attested in multiple sources. The date (334 BCE, autumn) is secure. The siege extended into 333 BCE.

events.title: Darius III mobilizes the Persian Empire's resources for war
events.description: In response to Alexander's invasion, Darius III Codomannus, the Achaemenid Great King, begins mobilizing the vast resources of the Persian Empire for a major counteroffensive. Darius had only recently secured his throne (336 BCE) and faced internal challenges, but Alexander's invasion forces him to prioritize external defense. Darius orders the assembly of a massive army, drawing troops from across the empire's provinces. He also sends the Greek mercenary general Memnon of Rhodes to command Persian naval forces and conduct a counter-campaign in the Aegean. This mobilization is significant because it represents the Persian Empire's commitment to defending itself against the Macedonian threat, though the empire's vast size and logistical challenges mean that concentrating forces takes time.
key_figures: Memnon of Rhodes (Greek mercenary general, Persian naval commander)
events.certainty_note: The mobilization is attested in sources, but the precise timing and extent are not documented in contemporary Persian records (which do not survive). The date is inferred from the narrative of events.

disconfirming_evidence: The casualty figures reported in ancient sources for the Battle of the Granicus are highly unreliable and vary wildly (from 1,000 to 20,000+ Persian casualties). Modern scholarship treats these numbers with skepticism. Additionally, some sources (particularly Curtius Rufus) contain dramatic embellishments and invented details (e.g., Alexander's near-drowning) that are not corroborated by earlier sources like Arrian. The claim that Alexander 'threw his spear' onto Asian soil is likely a later literary invention. The extent of Persian resistance and the actual strength of Persian forces in Asia Minor in 334 BCE are debated; some scholars argue that the satraps were not fully prepared for Alexander's invasion and that Darius's delayed response was partly due to underestimating the threat.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 334 BCE is rich but heavily dependent on sources written 300+ years after the events (Arrian, Plutarch, Diodorus, Curtius). These sources drew on earlier accounts by Ptolemy I and Aristobulus, who were participants in Alexander's campaign, but those earlier works survive only in quotation or paraphrase. No contemporary Persian sources survive; our understanding of the Persian perspective is filtered through Greek and later Hellenistic sources. The sources are generally favorable to Alexander and may exaggerate his achievements or minimize Persian capabilities. Archaeological evidence from Asia Minor provides some corroboration (destruction layers, inscriptions) but is limited. The narrative is shaped by the triumphalist tradition of Alexander's campaign and by the later Hellenistic historiographic tradition.

graph_edges.note: The crossing of the Hellespont directly led to the Battle of the Granicus within weeks.
Victory at Granicus enabled Alexander to advance south and besiege Miletus.
Fall of Miletus allowed Alexander to move further south to Halicarnassus.
Darius's mobilization resulted in the assembly of forces that would meet Alexander at Issus in 333 BCE.
Alexander's invasion triggered Darius's mobilization response.
